serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of sauteed pork balls with brown sauce is approximately 150 g, equivalent to 6–8 meatballs coated in soy-based brown glaze, as commonly served at a Chinese restaurant or hawker stall.

nutrient_source.title: Sauteed Pork Balls With Brown Sauce — HPB Singapore Foodo Database (G00010141)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database record G00010141 for 奶奶家焦溜丸子 Sauteed Pork Balls With Brown Sauce. All per_100g values are sourced from this record at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat is not reported in the HPB record and is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for pork meatball (NDB 10219) as a close analogue; documented in notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for 焦溜丸子 (deep-fried pork balls with brown sauce) in the University of Sydney GI Database. The HPB record reports carbohydrate_g as 0.0 g/100g, reflecting the meat component only and not accounting for the cornstarch coating and soy-sugar glaze present in the actual dish. Because the HPB-reported carbohydrate is 0.0 g/100g, the glycemic load per 150 g serving calculates to 0.0. A GI of 45 (low) is assigned as an analogous estimate based on the GI of pork-based meat dishes with starch coatings, referencing the University of Sydney GI Database entry for meat-based mixed dishes. Consumers should note that the actual dish contains cornstarch and added sugar in the sauce, which may elevate the effective GI above this estimate.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 1050 mg/100g — far exceeding the 600 mg/100g threshold — driven by soy sauce and dark soy sauce in the brown glaze
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit this dish; sodium at 1050 mg/100g is 1.75× the high-concern threshold, and a 150 g serving delivers approximately 1575 mg sodium, representing 68% of the 2300 mg daily limit in a single serving.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Cornstarch batter coating and sugar-containing brown sauce contribute refined carbohydrates and added sugar not captured in the HPB per_100g carbohydrate figure of 0.0 g
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should be cautious: while the HPB record reports 0 g carbohydrate per 100 g (reflecting the meat component), the actual dish contains refined starch from the cornstarch coating and added sugar in the soy glaze, which will raise postprandial blood glucose; request reduced sauce and lighter coating, and limit portion size.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium (1050 mg/100g), saturated fat from pork and deep-frying, and cholesterol at 89 mg/100g collectively elevate cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should avoid this dish due to the combination of very high sodium (1050 mg/100g), saturated fat from deep-fried pork, and dietary cholesterol at 89 mg/100g, all of which contribute to arterial stiffness, elevated LDL, and increased blood pressure.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) and pork delivers arachidonic acid, both amplifying inflammatory pathways in RA
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this dish; deep-frying generates AGEs that worsen joint inflammation, and pork is a source of arachidonic acid which promotes pro-inflammatory eicosanoid production — a steamed or braised preparation would be a lower-AGE alternative.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sauteed Pork Balls with Brown Sauce (焦溜丸子, jiāo liū wánzi) is a classic Chinese dish in which seasoned ground pork is shaped into balls, coated in a cornstarch batter, deep-fried until golden and lightly crispy on the outside, then tossed in a savoury-sweet brown sauce made from soy sauce, dark soy sauce, sugar, garlic, and ginger. The result is a meatball that is crisp on the exterior and tender within, glazed in an umami-rich, slightly sweet soy-based sauce. The dish is popular in Chinese home cooking and is served in Singapore Chinese restaurants and hawker-style establishments. Per 100 g, the HPB Singapore Foodo database records 171 kcal, 19.1 g protein, 10.5 g fat, and 0.0 g carbohydrate — the carbohydrate figure reflects the meat component only and does not capture the cornstarch coating or added sugar in the sauce. Sodium is very high at 1050 mg/100g due to soy sauce and dark soy sauce, making this dish unsuitable for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. Cholesterol is 89 mg/100g. The deep-frying process generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs), which are of concern for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis or inflammatory conditions. Despite its high protein content, the dish is clinically restricted for hypertension, cardiovascular disease, and should be consumed cautiously by individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to the hidden refined carbohydrates in the coating and sauce.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database record G00010141 at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as a close analogue and documented. GI is an analogous estimate as no direct measurement exists for this dish.
notes: carbohydrate_g is reported as 0.0 g/100g per the HPB source (G00010141); this reflects the meat component of the dish only and does not capture the cornstarch batter coating or added sugar in the brown sauce present in the actual preparation — confirmed zero per HPB source for the meat fraction. sugar_g is reported as 0.0 g/100g per HPB source for the same reason — confirmed zero per HPB source for the meat fraction. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 g/100g — naturally zero for a meat-based product with no vegetable components recorded in the HPB entry. saturated_fat_g (3.6 g/100g) is not reported in the HPB record and is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cooked pork meatball (NDB 10219) as the closest approved-source analogue; this is an approximation. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, vitamin panel, phosphorus, zinc, selenium, and moisture data for this specific dish are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database (G00010141) and are not available in USDA FoodData Central for an exact match as of 2026. GI is an analogous estimate (45, low) based on University of Sydney GI Database meat-dish analogues; no direct GI measurement exists for 焦溜丸子. The HPB-reported carbohydrate of 0.0 g/100g yields a glycemic load of 0.0 per 150 g serving, though the actual dish carbohydrate load is higher due to cornstarch and sauce sugar.
